analysis:
The strongest heuristics (1–5) share a clear structure: fast Pareto prefilter → compact, k‑proportional shortlist via cheap proxies (box‑volume ± scalarization) → exact greedy/CELF on the shortlist using cached singleton HVs (and selective pairwise HVs) → bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps with incremental updates and deterministic tie‑breaking. They emphasize: small shortlist size, vectorized proxies, lazy evaluation (timestamps/heap), and HV‑call budgeting/pruning.

Weaker variants (6,10,11–21 and some others) tend to reintroduce avoidable costs: heavy or random initialization, little or no caching, full‑set HV recomputations inside nested loops, repeated creation of pg.hypervolume objects, frequent Python‑level list/remove/set churn, and insufficient upper‑bound pruning. Common inefficiencies: recomputing diffs/proxy arrays per loop, not reusing precomputed singleton HVs, lack of CELF lazy re‑evaluation, and unbounded swap/greedy loops that explode HV calls. Tradeoffs are evident: more aggressive HV budgets can improve final selection but must be paid by tighter proxies, selective pairwise caches, and careful pruning to remain practical.

Actionable micro‑observations:
- Precompute and reuse arrays (diffs, rect_vol, scalarized scores) and singleton HVs; only compute pairwise HVs when shortlist small.
- Use CELF (heap + timestamps) to avoid recomputing marginal gains.
- Apply cheap upper bounds (box‑volume or singleton HV) to prune candidates before expensive HV calls.
- Cap swap iterations and HV calls; prefer deterministic tie‑breaking and reproducible RNG.
experience: Favor deterministic shortlists, cheap vectorized proxies, cached singleton (and tiny pairwise) HVs, and CELF lazy greedy with tight upper‑bound pruning and an explicit HV‑call budget. Add a small, cached incremental swap pass. Prioritize data reuse (precompute diffs/scores), avoid Python-level remove/linear searches in hot loops, and keep shortlist sizes O(k) to maximize HV value per expensive call.
current_reflection: Favor deterministic shortlists, cheap vectorized proxies, cached singleton (and tiny pairwise) HVs, and CELF lazy greedy with tight upper‑bound pruning and an explicit HV‑call budget. Add a small, cached incremental swap pass. Prioritize data reuse (precompute diffs/scores), avoid Python-level remove/linear searches in hot loops, and keep shortlist sizes O(k) to maximize HV value per expensive call.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Always filter dominated points, rank with a cheap vectorized proxy, and keep an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs and use CELF/timestamps to avoid repeat HV calls. Limit HV budget and swap iterations, reuse buffers (avoid vstack per-candidate), and only precompute pairwise HVs when shortlist is tiny. Prioritize deterministic tie-breaks and a small, well‑tuned shortlist for consistent high-quality tradeoffs.

Cache singleton HVs, precompute diffs/box volumes, and use sets/preallocated arrays to avoid Python-level copying and membership costs. Use CELF lazy updates with tight upper bounds (box or scalarized) and an explicit HV-call budget. Prefer a small, well-chosen shortlist (O(k)) with moderate weight vectors; do selective pairwise caching only when shortlist ≤ ~40. Tune swap and shortlist sizes as the primary knobs for quality vs cost.

Always prefilter to nondominated points, vectorize box/weight scores, and build an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only when shortlist≤~40). Use CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and box‑based upper bounds, limit total HV calls, maintain selected points in a preallocated array to avoid vstack churn, and use sets/masks for membership. Finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap pass. Consider lightweight scalar HVC approximations if HV calls must be avoided.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: deterministic, O(k) shortlist, cached singletons/pairwise HV, CELF lazy greedy, box/volume upper bounds, HV‑call budget, vectorized proxies, incremental swap
- Advice: Prefilter nondominated, build a small O(k) shortlist with cheap vectorized proxies, cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only when tiny), run CELF with timestamps + tight box upper bounds, cap HV calls, reuse precomputed diffs/scores/buffers, then do a tiny cached incremental swap; keep ordering stable.
- Avoid: Python-level remove/linear searches, per-candidate vstack/memory churn, large shortlists, unbounded HV recomputations, nondeterministic tie breaks.
- Explanation: These trade minimal exact HV calls for maximal marginal gain, yield reproducible high-quality k-subsets with predictable cost.
